categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Five regimes in 85 years — the long search for stable government

entries.label: The Restoration — Louis XVIII and Charles X
entries.hint: The Bourbon monarchy is restored after Waterloo — Louis XVIII governs as a constitutional monarch but Charles X attempts to reverse the Revolution; his July Ordinances (1830) suspending press freedom trigger the July Revolution that overthrows him in three days.
entries.note: Louis XVIII's Constitutional Charter 4 Jun 1814: Archives nationales. Charles X's July Ordinances 25 Jul 1830: Moniteur Universel 26 Jul 1830 (BnF). The July Revolution (les Trois Glorieuses) 27–29 Jul 1830: police and press records. Bertier de Sauvigny (1966), The Bourbon Restoration: the standard English study.

entries.label: Louis-Philippe — the bourgeois king
entries.hint: Louis-Philippe rules as a constitutional monarch under the July Monarchy — he is the 'bourgeois king' who carries an umbrella; the regime enriches the middle class but ignores workers; the Revolution of 1848 (February) overthrows him when he refuses electoral reform.
entries.note: Louis-Philippe's accession charter 9 Aug 1830: Archives nationales. His abdication 24 Feb 1848: documented in Archives nationales and press. Tocqueville's account of 24 Feb 1848: Souvenirs (written 1850, published 1893) — an eyewitness account. Pinkney (1972), The French Revolution of 1830: the standard study.

entries.label: Napoleon III — the Second Empire
entries.hint: Louis-Napoleon is elected President (December 1848), stages a coup (2 December 1851) and declares himself Emperor Napoleon III (2 December 1852); his Second Empire industrialises France and rebuilds Paris under Haussmann; defeated at Sedan (1870) he is captured and the Empire collapses.
entries.note: The coup of 2 Dec 1851: Archives nationales AF IV; Victor Hugo, Histoire d'un crime (written 1851–52, published 1877). The plebiscite of 20 Nov 1851: Interior Ministry records. Sedan and capture 2 Sep 1870: Archives de la Guerre A1. Price (1997), The French Second Empire: the standard study.

entries.label: Third Republic — France's longest-lasting democratic regime
entries.hint: The Third Republic is proclaimed on 4 September 1870 — it will last until 1940, France's most durable republican regime; the first two decades are dominated by the Commune (1871), monarchist threats and the Dreyfus Affair (1894–1906); the Constitutional Laws of 1875 are the founding texts.
entries.note: Proclamation of the Republic 4 Sep 1870: Moniteur Universel 5 Sep 1870 (BnF). Constitutional Laws of 1875: Journal Officiel 25–28 Feb and 17 Jul 1875. Gambetta's role: documented in his correspondence (Archives nationales 615AP). Mayeur & Rebérioux (1984), The Third Republic from its Origins to the Great War: the standard English study.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Revolutions, the Commune and colonial conquest

entries.label: Revolutions of 1830 and 1848 — the long revolutionary cycle
entries.hint: Paris has three revolutions in 18 years — July 1830 (overthrows Charles X), February 1848 (overthrows Louis-Philippe), June 1848 (workers suppressed); the barricades of Paris are the most documented street fighting in 19th-century Europe.
entries.note: July 1830 (Trois Glorieuses): police and press records, Archives nationales, série F7. February 1848: documented in the press — digitised Gallica. June 1848 suppression: Archives de la Guerre A1; 1,500 killed, 12,000 arrested documented. Tocqueville's eyewitness Souvenirs covers both. Marx, The Class Struggles in France (1850): a primary political analysis.

entries.label: The Paris Commune — 72 days of worker rule
entries.hint: After France's defeat by Prussia, Paris workers seize power for 72 days — the Commune establishes the world's first worker government; its suppression (la Semaine Sanglante, 21–28 May 1871) kills 10,000–30,000 Communards; the primary source archive is exceptionally rich.
entries.note: Commune Journal Officiel (18 Mar–24 May 1871): BnF Gallica. The Semaine Sanglante: Versailles army records (Archives de la Guerre A1). The 30,000 death estimate: Edwards (1971), The Paris Commune 1871. The photographic record: Commune photographs (BnF, département des estampes) — first major use of photography to document a political event. Marx, The Civil War in France (1871): written within weeks — primary political analysis.

entries.label: Franco-Prussian War — Sedan and French humiliation
entries.hint: Prussia defeats France in six weeks — the Emperor captured at Sedan (2 September 1870), Paris besieged and starved for 18 weeks; France cedes Alsace-Lorraine and pays 5 billion francs indemnity; the humiliation drives French politics for the next 44 years.
entries.note: Napoleon III's surrender at Sedan 2 Sep 1870: Archives de la Guerre A1. The siege of Paris: documented in the Journal officiel de la Défense nationale (BnF). The peace terms: Archives des Affaires Étrangères. Howard (1961), The Franco-Prussian War: the standard modern study. The loss of Alsace-Lorraine: documented in the Frankfurt Treaty 10 May 1871.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Constitutional instability and the search for a republic

entries.label: Constitutional Laws of 1875 — founding the Third Republic
entries.hint: Three constitutional laws are voted in 1875 — establishing the Senate, the Chamber of Deputies and the presidency; the Republic is confirmed by one vote (353 to 352) on 30 January 1875; this marginally-won republic lasts 65 years.
entries.note: Constitutional Laws: Journal Officiel 25–28 Feb and 17 Jul 1875. The one-vote margin: Archives parlementaires (Séances de 1875). MacMahon's 'seize mai' crisis 1877: documented in Journal Officiel and press (Gallica). Mayeur & Rebérioux (1984). The laws remain partially in force today through the 1958 Constitution's Article 89.

entries.label: The Dreyfus Affair — truth against the state
entries.hint: Captain Alfred Dreyfus, a Jewish officer, is falsely convicted of treason in 1894 — imprisoned on Devil's Island; Zola's J'Accuse…! (13 January 1898) exposes the cover-up; Dreyfus is exonerated in 1906; the affair defines the French left-right divide for the entire 20th century.
entries.note: The 1894 court martial: Service Historique de la Défense (Vincennes). Zola's J'Accuse…! (L'Aurore, 13 Jan 1898): BnF Gallica — full text. Dreyfus's own prison diary (Carnets de l'île du Diable): published 1894–1895; BnF. Zola's trial records: Archives nationales. Burns (2014), Dreyfus: A Family Affair: uses family archives. The affair's exoneration decision 12 Jul 1906: Journal Officiel.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: The Church-State battle and the road to laïcité

entries.label: The battle for laïcité — Jules Ferry's education laws
entries.hint: Jules Ferry's laws (1881–1886) make primary education free, compulsory and secular — crucifixes removed from state schools, religious teachers replaced by lay teachers; it is the most consequential secularisation of education in European history and the foundation of French laïcité.
entries.note: Loi Ferry 16 Jun 1881 (free schools): Journal Officiel 17 Jun 1881. Loi du 28 Mar 1882 (secular, compulsory education): Journal Officiel. Loi Goblet 30 Oct 1886 (lay teachers): Journal Officiel. Ferry's speeches: Archives nationales 56AP. Ozouf (1982), L'École, l'Église et la République: the standard study. The 1905 law on separation of Church and State extends this — documented in the folio that follows.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Delacroix, the Impressionists, Flaubert and Zola

entries.label: Géricault and Delacroix — Romanticism and revolution
entries.hint: Géricault's Raft of the Medusa (1819) and Delacroix's Liberty Leading the People (1830) are the defining Romantic paintings — both are responses to specific historical events (the Medusa shipwreck; the July Revolution); both are in the Louvre and both faced official resistance.
entries.note: Raft of the Medusa (1819): Louvre INV 4884. Salon reception: Moniteur Universel Aug 1819 (BnF). Liberty Leading the People (1830): Louvre RF129. Delacroix's own diary (1822–1863): BnF fonds français 13761 — the richest artist's diary of the 19th century. Athanassoglou-Kallmyer (2010), Géricault: the standard study.

entries.label: Impressionism — Monet, Renoir, Degas and the 1874 exhibition
entries.loc: Paris (35 boulevard des Capucines, studio of photographer Nadar)
entries.hint: The first Impressionist exhibition opens on 15 April 1874 — 30 artists including Monet, Renoir, Degas, Pissarro and Morisot show 165 works; the critic Leroy coins 'Impressionism' as a mockery of Monet's Impression, Sunrise; the exhibition is the founding moment of modern art.
entries.note: Exhibition catalogue (15 Apr–15 May 1874): BnF; reprinted in Rewald (1973). Leroy's review 25 Apr 1874 (Le Charivari): BnF Gallica. Monet's Impression, Sunrise (1872): Musée Marmottan Monet, Paris. Morisot's The Cradle (1872): Orsay — the only woman in the founding group. Rewald (1973), The History of Impressionism: the standard art-historical study.

entries.label: Flaubert's Madame Bovary and Baudelaire's Fleurs du Mal — prosecuted art
entries.hint: Madame Bovary and Les Fleurs du Mal are both published in 1857 and both prosecuted for obscenity — Flaubert is acquitted, Baudelaire convicted; the two trials are the defining moment of 19th-century literary freedom and document the state's attempt to control artistic expression.
entries.note: Flaubert's trial records: Tribunal Correctionnel de Paris, 29 Jan 1857 — Archives nationales. Baudelaire's trial 20 Aug 1857: Archives nationales. Both first editions 1857: BnF. Flaubert's acquittal: Moniteur Universel 8 Feb 1857 (BnF). Baudelaire's conviction: he was ordered to remove 6 poems; order lifted posthumously 1949. Richardson (1994), Baudelaire: the standard English biography.

entries.label: Berlioz and the Romantic symphony — Symphonie Fantastique (1830)
entries.hint: Hector Berlioz premieres the Symphonie Fantastique on 5 December 1830 — the most autobiographical symphony ever written (it depicts his obsessive love for actress Harriet Smithson); the programme notes are a primary source for Romantic aesthetic ideology.
entries.note: Premiere 5 Dec 1830: Conservatoire de Paris records. The original programme notes (written by Berlioz): BnF département de la musique. Berlioz's Mémoires (1870, published posthumously): BnF — one of the great musical memoirs. The autograph score: BnF, MS 1189. Cairns (1999), Berlioz, Vol.I: the standard biography.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Romanticism, positivism and the birth of sociology

entries.label: Saint-Simon, Fourier and the origins of socialism
entries.hint: Henri de Saint-Simon and Charles Fourier develop the first systematic socialist alternatives to capitalism in Paris — Saint-Simon's vision of a technocratic society managed by industrialists and scientists; Fourier's utopian communities (phalanstères); their ideas directly influence Marx.
entries.note: Saint-Simon's L'Industrie (1817): BnF. Fourier's Théorie des quatre mouvements (1808): BnF. Marx's notebooks citing Saint-Simon: MEGA (Marx-Engels Gesamtausgabe). Manuel (1956), The New World of Henri Saint-Simon: the standard study. Fourier's manuscripts: Archives nationales, 10AS (Fourier archive).

entries.label: Zola's Rougon-Macquart — the social novel as primary source
entries.hint: Émile Zola's 20-novel Rougon-Macquart cycle documents Second Empire and early Third Republic France from the slums to the stock exchange — each novel is based on documented research (miners' notebooks, department store records); they are simultaneously primary sources for social history.
entries.note: Zola's research notebooks: BnF, NAF 10303–10345 (Nouveaux Acquisitions Françaises) — published in Zola's preparatory dossiers. Germinal (1885) research notes: BnF NAF 10307–10309 (he spent a week in the Anzin mines). L'Assommoir (1877) first edition: BnF. Brown (1995), Zola: A Life: the standard English biography, uses BnF manuscript collection.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Industrialisation, Haussmann's Paris and the social question

entries.label: Haussmann rebuilds Paris — the first urban planning at scale
entries.hint: Prefect Georges-Eugène Haussmann demolishes medieval Paris and builds the boulevard system — 27,000 buildings demolished, 102,000 new buildings constructed; the population displaced from central Paris to the periphery creates the class geography that produces the Commune.
entries.note: Haussmann's Mémoires (3 vols, 1890–1893): the primary account — BnF. The Prefecture of the Seine's building records: Archives de Paris. Jordan (1995), Transforming Paris: the standard study. The 102,000 new buildings: documented in Prefecture records. Haussmann's own later admission that he demolished too much: Mémoires Vol.III. The financing method (controversial bonds): documented in Finance Ministry records.

entries.label: French industrialisation — railways and banking
entries.loc: France (railway network; Lyon silk; Nord coal)
entries.hint: France industrialises more slowly than Britain but the railway network (5,000km by 1860) and the Crédit Mobilier banking system transform the economy; the 1900 Exposition Universelle celebrates France as the world's second industrial power.
entries.note: Railway network statistics: Annuaire statistique de la France (INSEE historical series). Crédit Mobilier prospectus 1852: Archives nationales, F12. The 1900 Exposition records: Archives nationales F12 4310–4412. Landes (1969), The Unbound Prometheus: places French industrialisation in comparative context.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Colonial empire, the Suez Canal and French cultural diplomacy

entries.label: Suez Canal — de Lesseps and French engineering diplomacy
entries.hint: Ferdinand de Lesseps opens the Suez Canal on 17 November 1869 — a French engineering triumph connecting the Mediterranean and Red Sea; the opening ceremony attended by Empress Eugénie is documented in photographs and press dispatches; it transforms global trade and geopolitics.
entries.note: Opening ceremony 17 Nov 1869: Le Figaro and Le Temps (BnF Gallica). De Lesseps's papers: Archives nationales, Compagnie de Suez fonds. Edgar-Bonnet (1959), Ferdinand de Lesseps: the standard biography. The Suez Canal Company records: ANOM and private archives. The canal's design drawings: Archives nationales, Cartes et Plans.

entries.label: French colonial empire — Africa and Indochina
entries.hint: France builds its second colonial empire in North Africa (Algeria 1830), sub-Saharan Africa and Indochina — by 1900 France controls 10 million km² and 50 million colonial subjects; Algeria is the most important (100,000 settlers by 1870) and most violent (1 million Algerians killed 1830–1880, estimated).
entries.note: The Algeria conquest 5 Jul 1830: Archives de la Guerre A1. Colonial population statistics: Annuaire statistique de la France. Algerian death toll estimate: Kiernan (2007), Blood and Soil: a comparative genocide study. The ANOM (Aix-en-Provence): the primary archive for all French colonial records. Aldrich (1996), Greater France: the standard English survey of the colonial empire.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: France between German unification and the 20th century

entries.label: German unification — France's new neighbour
entries.hint: The German Empire is proclaimed in the Hall of Mirrors at Versailles on 18 January 1871 — deliberately in occupied French territory; France loses Alsace-Lorraine and pays 5 billion francs; Germany replaces France as Europe's dominant continental power.
entries.note: Wilhelm I's proclamation: documented in Bismarck's memoirs and German military records (Bundesarchiv-Militärarchiv). The deliberate choice of Versailles: Howard (1961). The Treaty of Frankfurt 10 May 1871: Archives des Affaires Étrangères. The 5 billion franc indemnity: French Treasury records. Taylor (1955), Bismarck: The Man and the Statesman: contextualises the proclamation.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: George Sand — novelist, bohemian and political actor
entries.hint: Aurore Dupin (George Sand) publishes under a male pseudonym, wears men's clothes, smokes, and conducts highly publicised affairs with Chopin and Musset — her 80 novels are primary sources for provincial life; she writes political manifestos during the 1848 Revolution.
entries.note: George Sand's correspondence (25,000 letters): BnF and Bibliothèque historique de la Ville de Paris. Her published correspondence: Lubin edition (26 vols, 1964–1995). Her political manifestos 1848: BnF. The pseudonym 'George Sand' first used 1832: publisher's records. Barry (1977), Infamous Woman: The Life of George Sand: the standard biography. Her relationship with Chopin: documented in both their correspondence.

entries.label: Verlaine and Rimbaud — documented queer literary lives
entries.hint: Paul Verlaine and Arthur Rimbaud's relationship (1871–1873) is the most documented same-sex relationship in 19th-century French literature — their correspondence, Verlaine's trial records (Brussels, 1873) and Rimbaud's Une Saison en Enfer document a relationship that produced some of the greatest French poetry.
entries.note: Verlaine's trial for shooting Rimbaud (Brussels, 10 Jul 1873): Belgian judicial archives. Verlaine's prison correspondence: BnF. Rimbaud, Une Saison en Enfer (1873): first edition, printed by Alliance Typographique, Brussels; BnF holds copies. Their correspondence: BnF. Starkie (1954), Arthur Rimbaud: the standard English biography. The relationship's documentation is unusually complete for the period.

entries.label: The Paris Commune and women's participation
entries.hint: Women play a central role in the Paris Commune — organising clubs, running ambulances, fighting on barricades; Louise Michel ('La Louve') is the most prominent; the photograph record of women Communards is the most detailed visual documentation of women in political action in 19th-century history.
entries.note: Louise Michel's testimony at her trial (Dec 1871): Archives nationales, Conseils de guerre. Her own Mémoires (1886): BnF. The photographs of women Communards (BnF département des estampes): the first major photographic documentation of women in armed political action. Thomas (1966), The Women Incendiaries: uses trial records to document women's roles. The 'pétroleuses' accusation (women allegedly burning Paris): documented as propaganda in press archives.
